cards.back: Earliest time an activity can start given predecessors are complete. Determined during forward pass.
Earliest time an activity can finish. Formula: EF = ES + Duration
Latest time an activity can start without delaying the project. Determined during backward pass.
Latest time an activity can finish without delaying the project. Formula: LS = LF - Duration
Amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project. Formula: Slack = LS - ES (or LF - EF)
The longest sequence of dependent activities where all have zero slack. Determines project duration. Any delay extends project.
Calculation that determines ES and EF for each activity, working left to right. Identifies earliest project finish date.
Calculation that determines LS and LF for each activity, working right to left. Identifies latest acceptable times.

cards.front: What is the merge point rule?
cards.back: When multiple activities converge, ES of next activity = maximum (latest) EF of all predecessors.

cards.front: What is the burst point rule?
cards.back: When one activity has multiple successors, LF = minimum (earliest) LS of all successors.
Activities that can run simultaneously, not dependent on each other. Reduce project timeline. Key to schedule compression.
Activities that must be done one after another. A must finish before B starts. Total duration is sum of all durations.

cards.front: How does Gantt chart differ from network diagram?
cards.back: Gantt shows activities as bars over time (easy to visualize). Network shows dependencies with arrows (identifies critical path). Use both.

cards.front: What is a project network diagram?
cards.back: Shows logical sequence and dependencies of activities. Built from WBS. Answers 'When must work be done?' and 'What depends on what?'
